1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	
4	Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $k$ in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus $g = g(C)$.
5	
6	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of $C$ in the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine $g$-dimensional abelian variety even when $C(k) = \emptyset$ (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus $0$ the witness object is instead the trivial scheme $\Spec k$, \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement} (\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}), whose committed proof route is the characteristic-free theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma chain of \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}, \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument.
7	
8	\section{The Albanese construction}
9	
10	For a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$, the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from $C$. Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
11	
12	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
13	
14	Let $C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)$ be the $g$\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of $C$ (the quotient of $C^g$ by the action of the symmetric group $S_g$). It is a smooth proper scheme over $k$ of dimension $g$. Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
15	\[
16	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
17	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
18	\]
19	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing $P_0$ gives a translation isomorphism $\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}$, and the Stein factorisation of $\sigma$ yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
20	
21	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of $C$ inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
22	
23	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
24	
25	\begin{definition}\leanok
26	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
27	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
28	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
29	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
30	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
31	  % such $b$ arises from a unique map $a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}$ such that
32	  % $a(0)=0$.  Since $B^*$ is integral, $a$ factors through $P$.  Thus the
33	  % maps $a\:B^*\to P$ and $b\:X\to B$ are in bijective correspondence.
34	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps $b'\:B\to B'$ such
35	  % that $b'(0)=0$.  In particular, $1_P$ corresponds to a natural map
36	  % $u\:X\to A$ such that $u(x)=0$, and every map $b\:X\to B$ factors
37	  % uniquely through $u$."
38	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
39	  Let $(C, P)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with a marked $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$. A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ is an \emph{Albanese object} for $(C, P)$ if there exists a morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ sending $P$ to the identity element $\eta \in J$, and such that every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ factors uniquely as $f = \iota \circ g$ for some morphism of group schemes $g \colon J \to A$.
40	\end{definition}
41	
42	\begin{remark}
43	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
44	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
45	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on $J$ --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on $\iota$ and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on $J$ sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on $\Jac(C)$ before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety $A$ in the universal-property clause.
46	\end{remark}
47	
48	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
49	
50	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term $h$ packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition $P \circ \iota = \eta_J$ and the universal-factorisation property of $\iota$. The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
51	
52	\begin{definition}
53	
54	\leanok
55	[The Albanese universal morphism]
56	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
58	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
59	  Given a pointed curve $(C, P)$ and an Albanese term $h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$, the \emph{universal morphism} of $h$ is the morphism
60	  \[
61	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
62	  \]
63	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of $h$.
64	\end{definition}
65	
66	\begin{lemma}
67	
68	\leanok
69	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
70	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
72	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
73	  The universal morphism $h.\mathtt{ofCurve}$ sends the marked point $P$ to the identity of $J$:
74	  \[
75	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
76	  \]
77	\end{lemma}
78	
79	\begin{lemma}
80	
81	\leanok
82	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
83	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
84	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
85	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
86	  For every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, there exists a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g$.
87	\end{lemma}
88	
89	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point $P \in C(k)$ as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that $P$, and applies the corresponding helper above.
90	
91	\begin{theorem}\leanok
92	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
93	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
95	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
96	  Let $J_1, J_2$ be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve $(C, P)$, with universal pointed morphisms $\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1$ and $\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2$ (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism $e \colon J_1 \to J_2$ satisfying $\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e$. The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation $\mathrm{id}$, and the produced morphism $e$ is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of $e$ as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
97	\end{theorem}
98	
99	\begin{remark}
100	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
101	  \uses{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
102	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism $h \colon J_2 \to J_1$ and verifies $g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}$ and $h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}$ (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple $(g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})$. The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
103	  \[
104	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
105	  \]
106	  The natural strengthening would be to return
107	  \[
108	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
109	  \]
110	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
111	\end{remark}
112	
113	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
114	
115	\begin{definition}\leanok
116	[Jacobian]
117	  \label{def:Jacobian}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
119	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
120	  The \emph{Jacobian} of $C$ is the abelian variety $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ obtained as the Albanese object of $C$: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ of dimension $g(C)$ equipped with a universal morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$. Formally, $\Jac(C)$ is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-$P$) Albanese witness for $C$ provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
121	
122	  When $g(C) = 0$ the Jacobian collapses to $\Spec k$ itself, which is the terminal object in the category of $k$-schemes. When $g(C) > 0$ the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers $C$ itself (after choosing a $k$-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: $\Jac(C)$ is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-$0$ specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over $k$ of relative dimension $0$ is isomorphic to $\Spec k$.
123	\end{definition}
124	
125	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
126	
127	The four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
128	
129	\begin{theorem}\leanok
130	[Group object structure]
131	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
133	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
134	  $\Jac(C)$ carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	\begin{proof}
138	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
139	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, $\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J$ as a $k$-scheme, where $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$ is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field $\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J$, so $\Jac(C)$ inherits it by projection.
140	
141	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if $\iota \colon C \to J$ is the universal morphism sending the marked point $P$ to the identity, then the diagonal $C \times C \to J \times J$, post-composed with the universal morphism out of $C \times C$ (factored through $J$ by Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
142	\end{proof}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}\leanok
145	[Smoothness and dimension]
146	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
148	  \uses{def:Jacobian, def:genus}
149	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Cor.~cor:sm (smoothness/dimension over a field) + Cor.~cor:ch0 (char-$0$ smoothness) + Prp.~prp:P0 (smooth over reduced $S$); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~34, 38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:sm): "Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.
151	  % Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$.  Then
152	  % $$\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).$$ Equality holds if and
153	  % only if $\IPic_{X/k}$ is smooth at $0;$ if so, then $\IPic_{X/k}$ is
154	  % smooth of dimension $\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})$ everywhere."
155	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:ch0): "Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.
156	  % Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$. If $k$ is
157	  % of characteristic $0$, then $\IPic_{X/k}$ is smooth of dimension
158	  % $\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})$ everywhere."
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:P0, smoothness clause): "Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists,
160	  % and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.  For $s\in S$, assume all the
161	  % $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ are smooth of the same dimension.  Then $\IPic_{X/S}$
162	  % has an open group subscheme $\IPicz_{X/S}$ of finite type whose fibers
163	  % are the $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$.  Furthermore, if $S$ is reduced, then
164	  % $\IPicz_{X/S}$ is smooth over $S$."
165	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor.~(cor:sm), Cor.~(cor:ch0), Prp.~(prp:P0).}
166	  $\Jac(C) \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$.
167	\end{theorem}
168	
169	\begin{proof}
170	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, def:genus}
171	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$; the relative dimension $g(C)$ is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
172	
173	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of $C$ equals the genus $g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to $\Jac(C)$ at the identity is canonically identified with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of $\mathcal O_C$ is classified by an element of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$, and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$. Smoothness of $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any $k$-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of $\Jac(C)$ at every $k$-rational point is the same as at the identity.
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\begin{theorem}\leanok

231	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
232	
233	\begin{itemize}
234	  \item $g = 0$: Then $\Jac(C) = \Spec k$, the trivial group scheme; $\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $0 = g$.
235	  \item $g = 1$: Then $\Jac(C) \cong C$ once a $k$-rational point on $C$ is fixed; $C$ is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension $1$).
236	  \item $g \ge 2$: $\Jac(C)$ is a non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to $C$ as a scheme.
237	\end{itemize}

239	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
240	
241	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.
242	
243	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
244	
245	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of $C$. This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.

247	\begin{definition}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[Albanese witness for a curve]
251	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}

253	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
254	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ is a bundle of the following seven fields:
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item $J$: an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$ --- the candidate Albanese object.
257	    \item $\mathtt{grpObj}$: a group-object structure on $J$ in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
258	    \item $\mathtt{proper}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is proper.
259	    \item $\mathtt{smooth}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth.
260	    \item $\mathtt{geomIrred}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
261	    \item $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$. This refines $\mathtt{smooth}$ at the specific relative dimension $\genus\, C$ (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
262	    \item $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$: a proof that, for every $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
263	  \end{itemize}
264	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
265	\end{definition}

267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
269	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
270	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
271	  \[
272	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
273	  \]
274	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single $J$ is chosen once (intrinsic to $C$), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
275	  \[
276	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
277	  \]
278	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to $C$: a different choice of $P \in C(k)$ alters the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism of $J$, and so leaves $J$ unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-$P$ projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, and $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ each take a marked point $P$ as input and project the corresponding component of $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P$ via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
279	\end{remark}

311	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-$0$ sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$ --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from $\bar k$ to the base field $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
312	
313	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a $P$-independent witness.}
314	  The witness $J$ depends only on $C$, not on the marked point $P$. Indeed, both classical constructions below produce $J$ as an intrinsic object of $C$ (the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$, or the Stein-factorisation target of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ translated by a fixed $P_0$); a different choice of $P$ in $C(k)$ alters $\iota_P$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism, and so does not change $J$ as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling $J$ as a single field and the universal-property data as $\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J$. We now turn to producing $J$.
315	
316	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
317	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
318	
319	  \begin{itemize}
320	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a $k$-scheme $T$, write $C_T := C \times_k T$ and let $\pi \colon C_T \to T$ be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
321	    \[
322	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
323	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
324	    \]
325	    A $T$-point is a line bundle on $C_T$ modulo a line bundle pulled back from $T$. The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C_T)$ descends to the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ and gives $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ a natural group-functor refinement.
326	
327	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For $C$ smooth proper geometrically connected over $k$ with a $k$-rational point, $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ (after \'etale sheafification, if $k$ is not algebraically closed) is representable by a $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k$, which is needed for the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ to be \'etale-locally injective.
328	
329	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ be the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$. As the identity component of a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is geometrically irreducible; for $C$ smooth proper of genus $g$, it is moreover a smooth proper $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$ is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the kernel of the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$, which is locally constant). Take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
330	
331	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each $P \in C(k)$, the Abel--Jacobi morphism
332	    \[
333	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
334	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
335	    \]
336	    sends $P$ to the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. It is universal among morphisms $f \colon C \to A$ to smooth proper geometrically irreducible $k$-group schemes $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$: this is the classical Albanese property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, proved by reducing to the case $k = \overline k$ (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on $C \times A$ trivialised along $\{P\} \times A$ correspond bijectively to morphisms $C \to A^{\vee} = A$ sending $P$ to $\eta_A$.
337	  \end{itemize}

339	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.

342	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$, which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of $\mathrm{Quot}$, hence $\mathrm{Hilb}$, as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}.
343	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction $G^0 \subseteq G$, and the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$.
344	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form.
345	  \end{itemize}

21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.

48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying

73	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
74	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
75	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
76	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
77	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
78	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
79	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
80	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
81	% assertion."
82	\begin{proof}
83	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
84	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
85	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
86	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
87	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
88	
89	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
90	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
91	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
92	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
93	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
94	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
95	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
96	
97	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
98	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
99	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
100	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
101	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
102	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
103	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
104	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
105	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
106	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{remark}
110	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
111	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
112	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
113	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
114	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
115	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
116	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
117	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
118	  irreducible.)
119	\end{remark}

121	\section{The theorem of the cube (deferred deep input)}
122	
123	\textbf{Deferred prerequisite.} The block below records the theorem of the cube as the heavy,
124	\emph{not-yet-built} characteristic-free input that the single-curve base case of
125	\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} ultimately rests on. We give Mumford's statement
126	verbatim but \emph{no} formal proof: in Mumford the proof spans \S6 (the splitting
127	$T(X_0 \times \cdots \times X_n) = \operatorname{Im}\alpha \oplus \operatorname{Ker}\beta$
128	exhibiting $\Pic$ as a quadratic functor, then the seesaw principle), and the
129	characteristic-free scheme-theoretic form is Chapter~III \S10. This is the one genuinely
130	deep, un-formalised prerequisite of route~(c); it carries no Lean target yet.
131	
132	\begin{theorem}
133	[Theorem of the cube]
134	  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
135	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM. Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties, $Z$ any variety and $x_0$,
137	  % $y_0$ and $z_0$ base points on $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, respectively. If $L$ is any line bundle
138	  % on $X \times Y \times Z$ whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$,
139	  % $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$ and $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, $L$ is trivial."
140	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S6, Theorem of the cube, p.~55.}
141	  Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties and $Z$ any variety over $\bar k$, with base points
142	  $x_0 \in X$, $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$. If $L$ is a line bundle on $X \times Y \times Z$
143	  whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$, $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$, and
144	  $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, then $L$ is trivial. \emph{(Recorded as a deferred
145	  input; no formal proof in this project yet --- this is the heavy un-built prerequisite of
146	  route~(c).)}
147	\end{theorem}

149	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
150	
151	\begin{proposition}
152	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
153	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
155	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
156	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
158	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
160	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
161	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
162	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
163	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
164	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
165	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
166	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
167	  single $\bar k$-point.
168	\end{proposition}
169	
170	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
171	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
172	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
173	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
174	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
175	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
176	\begin{proof}
177	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
178	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
179	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
180	
181	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
182	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
183	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
184	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
185	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
186	
187	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
188	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
189	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
190	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
191	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
192	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
193	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
194	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
195	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
196	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
197	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
198	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
199	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
200	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)
201	
202	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
203	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
204	  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
205	  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
206	  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
207	  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
208	\end{proof}
209	
210	\begin{remark}
211	  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
212	  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
213	  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
214	  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
215	  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
216	  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
217	  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
218	  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
219	  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
220	  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
221	  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
222	\end{remark}

224	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
225	
226	\begin{proposition}
227	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
228	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
229	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
230	  \uses{def:genus}
231	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
232	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
233	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
234	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
235	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
236	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
237	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
238	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
239	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
240	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
241	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
242	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
243	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
244	\end{proposition}
245	
246	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
247	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
248	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
249	\begin{proof}
250	  \uses{def:genus}
251	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
252	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
253	
254	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
255	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
256	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
257	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
258	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
259	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
260	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
261	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
262	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
263	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
264	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
265	\end{proof}
266	
267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
269	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
270	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
271	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
272	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
273	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
274	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
275	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
276	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
277	\end{remark}
278	
279	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
280	
281	\begin{theorem}
282	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
283	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
284	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
285	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
286	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
287	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
288	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
289	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
290	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
291	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
292	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
293	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
294	
295	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
296	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
297	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
298	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
299	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
300	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
301	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
302	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
303	  \eta[A]$.
304	\end{theorem}
305	
306	\begin{proof}
307	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
308	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
309	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
310	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
311	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
312	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
313	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
314	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
315	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
316	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
317	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
318	
319	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
320	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
321	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
322	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
323	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
324	\end{proof}

58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.

409	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality $f = c$ on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and $A$ is separated over $\bar k$ (proper implies separated), so a $\bar k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms for the open $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives $f = c$ as scheme morphisms.

To obtain the corresponding equality of morphisms over the base field $k$, we descend along the base change $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$.

413	      Concretely, let $f, c \colon C \to A$ be two $k$-morphisms (in the application $f$ is the given map and $c$ the constant map at $\eta_A$). Base-change along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ produces $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k} \colon C_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ together with the projections $p \colon C_{\bar k} = C \times_k \bar k \to C$ and $q \colon A_{\bar k} \to A$, fitting the base-change squares $q \circ f_{\bar k} = f \circ p$ and $q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$. Suppose $f_{\bar k} = c_{\bar k}$ (this is what \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} delivers over $\bar k$). Then $f \circ p = q \circ f_{\bar k} = q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$, so $f$ and $c$ agree after pre-composition with $p$.

415	      It remains to right-cancel $p$. The morphism $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat (a field extension is a faithfully flat ring map) and surjective; both properties are stable under base change, so the projection $p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C$ is faithfully flat and surjective. By \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}, $p$ is therefore an epimorphism of schemes, and right-cancelling it in $f \circ p = c \circ p$ gives $f = c$ over $k$. This is the entire descent: a faithfully-flat surjective cover induces an epimorphism, hence the base-change map is injective on the $\mathrm{Hom}$-set into $A$. The step is a $\sim 2$-line consequence of the named Mathlib lemma plus base-change-square commutativity, and is cheaper than the proper-cohomology flat-base-change machinery a cohomological descent would have required.

506	\begin{proof}
507	  \uses{lem:rigidity_theorem}
508	  After extending scalars to $\bar k = k^{al}$ (the hypotheses persist), assume $k$ algebraically closed; $V$ is then connected. Let $U_0$ be an open affine neighbourhood of $u_0$. By completeness of $V$ the projection $q \colon V \times W \to W$ is closed, so $Z := q(\alpha^{-1}(U \smallsetminus U_0))$ is closed in $W$, and $w \notin Z \iff \alpha(V \times \{w\}) \subseteq U_0$. Since $w_0 \notin Z$, the open set $W \smallsetminus Z$ is non-empty; for $w \in W \smallsetminus Z$ the image $\alpha(V \times \{w\})$ is a complete subset of the affine $U_0$, hence a single point, namely $\alpha(v_0, w) = u_0$. Thus $\alpha$ is constant on the dense open $V \times (W \smallsetminus Z)$ of the irreducible $V \times W$; as $U$ is separated, $\alpha$ agrees with the constant map $u_0$ on all of $V \times W$.

453	The chain of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- cube-free and cohomology-free --- and supplies the additive/multi-factor structure. The \emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters as the genuinely deep, characteristic-free input supplying the single-curve base case that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$; the cube is \textbf{required} here --- the Rigidity Lemma alone does \emph{not} establish that base case. 455	\begin{theorem}\leanok
456	[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
457	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
458	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
459	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar, def:genus}
460	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is $\Spec k$ (the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $0$, properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field encodes the Albanese universal property for every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$.
461	\end{theorem}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, def:genus}
465	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
466	
467	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take $J := \mathbf{1}$, the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, which on underlying schemes is $\Spec k$ viewed as a $k$-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
468	
469	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to $\mathbf{1}$, and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism $\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}$ is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:120}--\texttt{126}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on $\Spec k$ from the fact that, on the geometric fibre $\Spec K$ over any field extension $k \to K$, the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and $\Spec K$ is irreducible.
470	
471	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension $\genus\,C$.} The smoothness witness $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is required at relative dimension $\genus\,C$. The hypothesis $\genus\,C = 0$ rewrites the target relative dimension to $0$, after which the goal reduces to $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})$. This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension $0$.
472	
473	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$, we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple $(C, P, J)$.
474	
475	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map $\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}$ (the structure morphism of $C$ as a $k$-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition $P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}$ holds automatically: both sides are morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
476	
477	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to A$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, we must exhibit a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = \alpha \circ g$. \emph{Existence}: take $g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$. Then $\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$, which equals $f$ precisely when $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. We obtain this equality by descent from the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$. Base-change the data along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$: the curve $C_{\bar k}$ is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible of genus $0$ (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve), the group scheme $A_{\bar k}$ is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible, the base-changed point $P_{\bar k}$ is a $\bar k$-rational point, and $f_{\bar k}$ still kills it: $f_{\bar k}(P_{\bar k}) = \eta_{A_{\bar k}}$. By \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, currently carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$, the latter removable once the characteristic-free route~(c) of \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c} is formalised), $f_{\bar k}$ equals the constant morphism $\mathtt{toUnit}\,C_{\bar k} \circ \eta_{A_{\bar k}}$ over $\bar k$. Finally we descend this morphism equality back to $k$: the projection $p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C$ is the base change of the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$, hence faithfully flat and surjective, hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; right-cancelling $p$ against the base-change-square identities yields $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$ as $k$-scheme morphisms (Sub-step~C.2.f of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}). \emph{Uniqueness}: any two morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to A$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ agreeing on $P \circ (-) = \eta_A$ are equal by the universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ as a category (a morphism out of $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator, here pinned to $\eta_A$).
478	
479	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch.} When no $k$-rational point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$ exists (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier $\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$ is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's $\forall$ over an empty type is trivially true. The construction handles this branch implicitly without any explicit case split.
480	
481	  \paragraph{Body closure status.} The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness} (in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean}) is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is gated on the body of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (M2.a), whose \textbf{committed} proof route is route~(c) --- the characteristic-free theorem-of-the-cube $\to$ rigidity-lemma chain of \cref{sec:av_rigidity_route_c} (headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}), which is decoupled from both Route~A representability and Serre duality. 482	\end{proof}
